participants.name: Hassan
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-xc6rc28l
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-xc6rc28l
participants.professional_persona: Hassan is a senior manager at a precision engineering firm that adopted AI for predictive maintenance via the SMEs Go Digital programme. He now champions AI literacy among his blue‑collar colleagues.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Lean manufacturing expertise, hands‑on with IoT sensor data integration, and basic Python scripting for dashboards.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: He aims to become his company’s chief digital officer and replicate the AI upskilling model across the manufacturing cluster.

participants.name: I
participants.professional_persona: I am an AI engineer in a health-tech startup, building diagnostic support tools that assist radiologists. Through the AI Trailblazers program we prototyped a generative model for medical report summarisation.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in computer vision, natural language processing, and model deployment on cloud platforms like AWS and GCP.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: My goal is to lead a cross-functional team that brings responsible AI solutions into clinical practice across Singapore's public hospitals.

participants.name: Chen
participants.professional_persona: Chen Wei is a research scientist at a multinational corporation's AI lab in Singapore, focusing on reasoning AI for complex decision-making. He previously led a team that developed a state-of-the-art theorem-proving model.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in neuro-symbolic reasoning, logical inference, and reinforcement learning with practical experience in enterprise automation.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To create AI systems that can reason transparently and robustly for high-stakes industry applications.

participants.name: Dr. (mi2gyg)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Nalini works as an independent AI researcher and consultant, collaborating with SMEs on bespoke AI solutions via the 100 Experiments programme. She brings deep experience from her previous role at a top-tier university.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Skilled in natural language processing, time-series forecasting, and building custom AI pipelines for logistics and finance.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To help small and medium enterprises adopt AI affordably and effectively, bridging the gap between research and practical deployment.

participants.name: Yue
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-wn16m030
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-wn16m030
participants.professional_persona: Yue Ling is a digital marketing specialist at a retail chain who participated in a hackathon to design an AI tool that reduces food waste in grocery stores. She is now part of her company’s AI task force.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Skilled in customer analytics, A/B testing, and using low‑code AI platforms; strong understanding of consumer behaviour.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: She wants to lead sustainability‑focused AI projects and eventually consult for retailers on using AI for public good outcomes.

The policy explicitly commits over S$500 million for AI R&D, which I take as the total funding envelope. The 100 Experiments programme caps co-funding at S$330,000 per project, which is reasonable for applied AI projects. For compute, given Singapore's push to provide high-performance infrastructure and the scale of 100E, I estimate around 500,000 GPU hours available annually for experimentation, based on typical allocations for national AI programmes. For trainers, I estimate 50 AI experts and trainers, drawing from AISG's network and the need to support both enterprises and apprentices.

initial_posts.narrative: From my perspective as a digital marketing specialist on my company's AI task force, I've seen firsthand how the 100 Experiments programme works. With S$500M funding and 50 trainers, the outreach sessions (40) and matchmaking events (12) should generate enough problem statements to approve 100 projects in 2023, as the programme name suggests. However, not all approved projects will result in a working prototype due to technical challenges and timeline constraints; I estimate about 80 prototypes are built. For trainees, the AI Apprenticeship Programme runs 9-month cycles; with 20 workshops and 60 onboarding assists, I expect around 150 trainees to complete the programme in 2023, assuming each workshop trains about 7-8 people and some drop out. Finally, about 70 enterprises will have at least one AI solution developed, as some enterprises may have multiple projects or prototypes but not all will reach a deployable stage within the year. My estimates are based on my company's experience with the programme and typical project success rates in retail AI.
evidence: The 100 Experiments programme is designed to fund 100 projects, and with 40 outreach sessions and 12 matchmaking events, there should be enough quality proposals to fill all slots.
Based on my company's hackathon experience, about 80% of approved AI projects yield a working prototype within the year due to technical hurdles and resource constraints.
With 20 training workshops and 60 onboarding assists, and assuming each workshop trains 10-15 people with a 50-70% completion rate, I estimate 150 trainees finish the programme.
From our AI task force, we've seen that about 70% of enterprises with approved projects end up with a usable AI solution, so 70 out of 100 enterprises should have at least one solution.

initial_posts.narrative: As an AI research scientist, I see the 100E programme as a structured pipeline from problem statements to prototypes. With S$500M funding and 50 trainers, the 40 outreach sessions and 12 matchmaking events should generate enough quality proposals to approve 100 projects in 2023, matching the programme's name. However, not all approved projects will yield working prototypes due to technical complexity and timeline constraints; based on my experience in enterprise AI, about 75% succeed, giving 75 prototypes. For trainees, the 20 workshops and 60 onboarding assists, combined with the 9-month AIAP cycle, suggest around 140 completions (accounting for attrition). Finally, about 65 enterprises will have at least one AI solution, as some enterprises run multiple projects but not all reach deployment. Yue's estimate of 80 prototypes is slightly optimistic; I think 75 is more realistic given typical R&D failure rates in applied AI.

Public trust in AI systems is at 65%, as successful deployments in government services (e.g., fraud detection, chatbots) build confidence, but high-profile failures or privacy concerns keep trust moderate.
